section.title: Solomon's Decline and Death
section.sectionRationale: Chapter 10 displayed Solomon's glory at its height, wisdom, wealth, and honor from the nations, while quietly cataloguing the very horses, gold, and growing household the law had warned a king against. Chapter 11 shows the cost. Solomon's many foreign wives turn his heart after other gods, the LORD announces that the kingdom will be torn away though one tribe is spared for David's sake, and adversaries rise on every side, Hadad, Rezon, and finally Jeroboam, to whom the prophet Ahijah tears a cloak into twelve pieces as a sign. The chapter ends with Solomon's death after forty years, closing the account of his reign with judgment already set in motion.
section.thesis: Solomon's many wives turn his heart after other gods, so that the LORD who twice appeared to him now announces the tearing of the kingdom, tempering judgment with mercy for David's sake and Jerusalem's, while raising up adversaries who make plain that no earthly king, however wise, keeps his heart whole without covenant faithfulness.
section.frameNote: Five beats: Solomon's foreign wives turn his heart to other gods, verses 1 to 8; the LORD's anger and the announcement that the kingdom will be torn away, verses 9 to 13; Hadad the Edomite raised up as an adversary, verses 14 to 22; Rezon of Aram and the rise of Jeroboam, verses 23 to 40; and the death of Solomon after forty years, verses 41 to 43.

verses.text: King Solomon, however, loved many foreign {{nashim|women}} along with the daughter of Pharaoh, women of Moab, Ammon, Edom, and Sidon, as well as Hittite women.

These women were from the nations about which the LORD had told the Israelites, “You must not intermarry with them, for surely they will turn your hearts after their gods.” Yet Solomon clung to these women in love.
He had seven hundred wives of royal birth and three hundred concubines, and his wives turned his heart away.
For when Solomon grew old, his wives turned his {{lev|heart}} after other gods, and he was not wholeheartedly devoted to the LORD his God, as his father David had been.
Solomon followed Ashtoreth the goddess of the Sidonians and Milcom the abomination of the Ammonites.
So Solomon did evil in the sight of the LORD; unlike his father David, he did not follow the LORD completely.
At that time on a hill east of Jerusalem, Solomon built a high place for Chemosh the abomination of Moab and for Molech the abomination of the Ammonites.
He did the same for all his foreign wives, who burned incense and sacrificed to their gods.

units.label: God's anger and the tearing of the kingdom
units.note: one tribe spared for David's sake

verses.text: Now the LORD grew angry with Solomon, because his heart had turned away from the LORD, the God of Israel, who had appeared to him twice.
Although He had warned Solomon explicitly not to follow other gods, Solomon did not keep the LORD's command.

Then the LORD said to Solomon, “Because you have done this and have not kept My {{berit|covenant}} and My statutes, which I have commanded you, I will tear the kingdom away from you and give it to your servant.
Nevertheless, for the sake of your father David, I will not do it during your lifetime; I will tear it out of the hand of your son.
Yet I will not tear the whole kingdom away from him. I will give one {{shevet|tribe}} to your son for the sake of My servant David and for the sake of Jerusalem, which I have chosen.”
Then the LORD raised up against Solomon an adversary, Hadad the Edomite, from the royal line of Edom.
Earlier, when David was in Edom, Joab the commander of the army had gone to bury the dead and had struck down every male in Edom.
Joab and all Israel had stayed there six months, until he had killed every male in Edom.
But Hadad, still just a young boy, had fled to Egypt, along with some Edomites who were servants of his father.
Hadad and his men set out from Midian and went to Paran. They took men from Paran with them and went to Egypt, to Pharaoh king of Egypt, who gave Hadad a house and land and provided him with food.
There Hadad found such great favor in the sight of Pharaoh that he gave to him in marriage the sister of Queen Tahpenes, his own wife.
And the sister of Tahpenes bore Hadad a son named Genubath. Tahpenes herself weaned him in Pharaoh's palace, and Genubath lived there among the sons of Pharaoh.
When Hadad heard in Egypt that David had rested with his fathers and that Joab, the commander of the army, was dead, he said to Pharaoh, “Let me go, that I may return to my own country.”
But Pharaoh asked him, “What have you lacked here with me that you suddenly want to go back to your own country?” “Nothing,” Hadad replied, “but please let me go.”

units.label: Rezon and the rise of Jeroboam
units.note: the kingdom will be given to ten tribes

verses.text: And God raised up against Solomon another adversary, Rezon the son of Eliada, who had fled from his master, Hadadezer king of Zobah,
and had gathered men to himself. When David killed the Zobaites, Rezon captained a band of raiders and went to Damascus, where they settled and gained control.
Rezon was Israel's enemy throughout the days of Solomon, adding to the trouble caused by Hadad. So Rezon ruled over Aram with hostility toward Israel.
Now Jeroboam son of Nebat was an Ephraimite from Zeredah whose mother was a widow named Zeruah. Jeroboam was a servant of Solomon, but he rebelled against the king,
and this is the account of his rebellion against the king. Solomon had built the supporting terraces and repaired the gap in the wall of the city of his father David.
Now Jeroboam was a mighty man of valor. So when Solomon noticed that the young man was industrious, he put him in charge of the whole labor force of the house of Joseph.

During that time, the prophet Ahijah the Shilonite met Jeroboam on the road as he was going out of Jerusalem. Now Ahijah had wrapped himself in a new cloak, and the two of them were alone in the open field.
And Ahijah took hold of the new cloak he was wearing, tore it into twelve pieces,
and said to Jeroboam, “Take ten pieces for yourself, for this is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: ‘Behold, I will tear the kingdom out of the hand of Solomon, and I will give you ten tribes.
But one tribe will remain for the sake of My servant David and for the sake of Jerusalem, the city I have chosen out of all the tribes of Israel.

For they have forsaken Me to worship Ashtoreth the goddess of the Sidonians, Chemosh the god of the Moabites, and Milcom the god of the Ammonites. They have not walked in My ways, nor done what is right in My eyes, nor kept My statutes and judgments, as Solomon's father David did.

Nevertheless, I will not take the whole kingdom out of Solomon's hand, because I have made him ruler all the days of his life for the sake of David My servant, whom I chose because he kept My commandments and statutes.
But I will take ten tribes of the kingdom from the hand of his son and give them to you.
I will give one tribe to his son, so that My servant David will always have a lamp before Me in Jerusalem, the city where I chose to put My Name.
But as for you, I will take you, and you shall reign over all that your heart desires, and you will be king over Israel.

If you listen to all that I command you, walk in My ways, and do what is right in My sight in order to keep My statutes and commandments as My servant David did, then I will be with you. I will build you a lasting dynasty just as I built for David, and I will give Israel to you.
Because of this, I will humble David's descendants, but not forever.'”
Solomon therefore sought to kill Jeroboam. But Jeroboam arose and fled to Egypt, to Shishak king of Egypt, where he remained until the death of Solomon.

units.label: The death of Solomon
units.note: forty years, then his son reigns

verses.text: As for the rest of the acts of Solomon, all that he did, as well as his wisdom, are they not written in the Book of the Acts of Solomon?
Thus the time that Solomon reigned in Jerusalem over all Israel was forty years.
And Solomon rested with his fathers and was buried in the city of his father David. And his son Rehoboam reigned in his place.

nashim.gloss: Women, wives, the plural of ishah. Verse 1 names the nashim nokriyot, foreign women, from the very nations Israel was told not to intermarry with, Deuteronomy 7:3-4, setting up the whole chapter's account of a heart divided by these unlawful unions.

lev.gloss: Heart, the seat of will, devotion, and allegiance. Verse 4 says his wives turned his lev after other gods, the same word used in chapter 10 of the wisdom God had placed in that heart, showing how a heart once gifted can still be led astray.

berit.gloss: Covenant, a binding agreement. The LORD charges Solomon with not keeping His berit and statutes, verse 11, the Davidic and Sinai obligations that bound the king to exclusive worship and covenant loyalty; breaking it triggers the announced tearing of the kingdom.

shevet.gloss: Tribe, or a rod or staff used to mark tribal identity. The LORD promises to leave one shevet for Solomon's son for David's sake, verse 13, and Ahijah's torn cloak enacts the same word in symbol, ten pieces for Jeroboam and one tribe remaining for the house of David.

basic.context: Solomon, whose wisdom and wealth were unmatched, loved many foreign women in direct violation of God's command, and in old age they turned his heart to worship their gods, including building high places for Chemosh and Molech. The LORD, angered that Solomon broke the covenant despite two personal appearances, announces that the kingdom will be torn from his line, though for David's sake this will not happen in Solomon's lifetime, and one tribe will remain for his son. Adversaries rise up, Hadad from Edom and Rezon from Aram, and finally the prophet Ahijah tears his cloak into twelve pieces before Jeroboam, a servant of Solomon, announcing that ten tribes will be given to him. Solomon dies after forty years, and Rehoboam succeeds him. The key questions are why a king given such wisdom fell so completely, how judgment and mercy hold together in God's announcement, and what this means for how a life gifted by God can still end in decline.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recounting a real decline and a real prophetic word, not a general formula that marriage itself is dangerous. The text is explicit: the danger was intermarriage with idolatrous nations against God's direct command, Deuteronomy 7:3-4, and the fruit was divided worship, not marriage as such. The chapter also raises the fair question of how God's sovereignty relates to human adversaries and Solomon's own choices, which needs careful handling so both divine sovereignty and human responsibility are honored.
basic.originalLanguages: Solomon loved many foreign nashim, women, from nations Israel was forbidden to intermarry with, verse 1. In old age these wives turned his lev, his heart, after other gods, verse 4, so that the LORD charges him with breaking His berit, covenant, verse 11. Yet mercy remains woven through judgment: one shevet, tribe, is preserved for David's sake and Jerusalem's, verse 13, a promise reenacted in the prophet's torn cloak.
basic.typology: Solomon's divided heart, wise yet unfaithful, anticipates every human king's failure to keep covenant perfectly, intensifying the reader's longing for a king whose heart never turns. The sparing of one tribe for David's sake, preserving a lamp in Jerusalem, foreshadows how God keeps His covenant promise alive through every human failure until it is fulfilled in David's greater Son.
basic.christ: Solomon, given wisdom beyond all others, still let his heart be turned by love disordered toward what God had forbidden; Jesus, tempted in every way as we are, never sinned, Hebrews 4:15, and unlike Solomon never built a single high place to a rival god. The one tribe preserved for David's sake, verse 13, keeps a lamp burning in Jerusalem until the true Son of David comes, of whom the angel says His kingdom will never end, Luke 1:32-33.
basic.god: God is patient, having appeared to Solomon twice with clear warning before finally acting in judgment, verse 9, showing He is slow to anger even with a covenant-breaking king. He is also just, refusing to overlook divided worship even in the wisest and wealthiest of kings. And He is faithful to His promise to David, tempering deserved judgment with mercy, sparing one tribe and delaying the tearing of the kingdom for the sake of a covenant made generations before.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 7:3-4 and Deuteronomy 17:17 forbid intermarriage with idolatrous nations and warn a king against many wives, precisely what Solomon violates. Second Samuel 7 records the covenant with David that explains why judgment is delayed and a remnant preserved. First Kings 12 shows the tearing of the kingdom actually occurring under Rehoboam. And Hebrews 4:15 contrasts every failed human king with Christ, who was tempted yet never sinned.
basic.oneStory: The wisest king Israel ever had could not keep his own heart whole once he set it on what God had forbidden, and the kingdom he received begins to unravel even before he dies. Yet even in judgment, God keeps His promise to David alive, sparing one tribe and one lamp in Jerusalem. The whole account presses toward the true Son of David whose heart never turns and whose kingdom, unlike Solomon's, will never be torn away.

context.literary: The chapter moves in a clear arc from indictment, verses 1 to 8, to divine sentence, verses 9 to 13, to a lengthening list of raised-up adversaries, verses 14 to 40, before closing with the formal notice of Solomon's death, verses 41 to 43, a pattern typical of the closing regnal formula used throughout Kings. The repeated phrase tear the kingdom, verses 11, 12, 13, 31, 34, 35, binds the sections together and signals that this chapter's real subject is the unraveling that chapter 10's glory was quietly setting in motion.
context.historical: Political marriages sealing alliances with neighboring rulers, Egypt, Moab, Ammon, Edom, Sidon, and the Hittites, were standard royal diplomacy in the ancient Near East, which is precisely why the practice was so tempting and so forbidden for Israel's king, whose loyalty belonged to the LORD alone. Ahijah's torn garment enacts a well-attested prophetic sign-act, and the raising of foreign adversaries, Hadad in Edom and Rezon in Damascus, reflects the historical reality that Solomon's peaceful, prosperous reign, chapter 4, was already eroding at its edges by its final years.
context.canonical: This chapter is the hinge between the united monarchy and the divided kingdom, explaining theologically why the ten northern tribes will break away in chapter 12: not political accident but the LORD's own righteous response to covenant unfaithfulness, foretold here through Ahijah. It also secures the Davidic promise of 2 Samuel 7 across the coming centuries of division and exile, since one tribe and one lamp in Jerusalem remain, keeping alive the line that leads to Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show precisely how and why Solomon's reign, marked by unmatched wisdom, ended in covenant unfaithfulness and provoked divine judgment, while demonstrating that God's mercy toward David's line kept His covenant promise alive even through deserved discipline.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Solomon's specific violations, intermarriage with idolatrous nations and the building of high places, and a specific prophetic announcement of judgment; it does not prescribe that every foreign marriage or every reversal in life is divine punishment. What endures as timeless truth is that divided worship provokes God's righteous anger, and that His covenant mercy persists even when judgment is deserved and delayed rather than cancelled.

debates.question: Is Solomon's inclusion of foreign wives an outright rejection of God, or a slow compromise that hardened over decades?

views.label: A gradual, cumulative compromise
views.case: Verse 4 specifies that it was 'when Solomon grew old' that his wives turned his heart, and the buildup of seven hundred wives and three hundred concubines suggests a slow accumulation of unlawful alliances rather than a single decisive act of apostasy.

views.label: A decisive violation from the outset
views.case: Verses 1 and 2 state plainly that Solomon loved these women in direct defiance of the LORD's explicit command not to intermarry with these nations, so the sin begins the moment the first forbidden marriage is made, regardless of how long the consequences take to fully unfold.

debates.bearing: Either way, the text holds Solomon responsible for choices made across his reign, showing that unfaithfulness can be both a single wrong step and a slow erosion, and that neither excuses covenant unfaithfulness before God.

debates.question: How can God announce that He raised up adversaries against Solomon, verses 14 and 23, while also holding Solomon fully responsible for his own sin?

views.label: God's sovereign use of secondary causes
views.case: Hadad and Rezon act from their own motives, revenge and ambition, yet the text says the LORD raised them up, showing that God sovereignly directs real historical agents and events to accomplish His righteous purposes without forcing their wills.

views.label: Prophetic judgment language describing consequence
views.case: Ancient Near Eastern historiography commonly attributed the natural unraveling of a kingdom's fortunes, weakened alliances, resentful neighbors, to divine action, so the language affirms that these adversaries are the real-world fruit of Solomon's failures rather than arbitrary new punishments.

debates.bearing: Both readings uphold that God is sovereign over history even as human agents remain morally responsible for their actions, a pattern consistent with the rest of Scripture's treatment of divine sovereignty and human choice.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's vocabulary traces Solomon's fall precisely. He loved many foreign nashim, women, verse 1, precisely the kind of nokriyot the law forbade Israel to marry. Age, not youth, is when these wives turn his lev, his heart, after other gods, verse 4, echoing chapter 10's note that God had placed chokmah in that same heart. The LORD's charge is that Solomon has not kept His berit, covenant, and statutes, verse 11, invoking the full weight of Sinai and the Davidic promise together. Yet mercy remains: one shevet, tribe, verse 13, is kept for David's sake, a promise reenacted visually when Ahijah tears his cloak into twelve pieces, nine plus one for Jeroboam and one remaining for the house of David.

typology.type: A wise king whose heart is nonetheless turned away by disordered love
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Son of David, tempted in every way yet without sin, whose heart never turns from the Father, Hebrews 4:15, Hebrews 7:26.

typology.type: One tribe and a lamp preserved in Jerusalem for David's sake despite deserved judgment
typology.fulfillment: God's unbroken covenant faithfulness to David's line through exile and division until it is fulfilled in Jesus, of whose kingdom there will be no end, Luke 1:32-33.

typology.type: A torn garment announcing the tearing of the kingdom
typology.fulfillment: The kingdom of God torn from unfaithful stewards and given to those who will bear its fruit, a pattern Jesus applies to the religious leaders of His own day, Matthew 21:43.

study.christ: Solomon possessed wisdom beyond any man, yet his own heart was turned by love set on what God had forbidden, proving that no merely human king, however gifted, can keep covenant faithfulness by wisdom alone. Jesus, tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin, Hebrews 4:15, is the true and better Solomon whose heart never divides its worship and who builds no high place to any rival god. And where the LORD preserved one tribe and one lamp in Jerusalem for David's sake despite Solomon's failure, verse 13, He was keeping the covenant promise alive across the coming centuries until it was fulfilled in great Son of David whose throne, unlike Solomon's, will never be torn away, Luke 1:32-33.
study.god: God is patient, appearing to Solomon twice with explicit warning before finally pronouncing judgment, verse 9, showing that His anger is neither quick nor careless. He is just, refusing to overlook divided worship even from the son of David and the builder of His own temple. And He is unshakably faithful to His covenant, tempering deserved judgment with mercy for David's sake, sparing one tribe and delaying the tearing of the kingdom so that His promise might endure even through human failure, ultimately reaching its fulfillment in Christ.

crossReferences.note: Israel is commanded not to intermarry with the surrounding nations because they will turn hearts after other gods, precisely what Solomon's wives do.
The king is warned not to acquire many wives, lest his heart turn away, a warning this chapter shows fulfilled in Solomon.
The Davidic covenant promising an enduring house and throne, the reason judgment is delayed and one tribe preserved.
The tearing of the kingdom announced here through Ahijah is carried out under Rehoboam.
Christ was tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin, unlike Solomon whose heart was turned.

study.oneStory: The wisest and wealthiest king Israel ever had could not keep his own heart from being divided once he set his love on what God had forbidden, and the united kingdom he inherited begins to unravel even before his death. Yet even as judgment falls, the LORD keeps His covenant promise to David alive, sparing one tribe and one lamp in Jerusalem for the sake of a promise made generations earlier. The whole account drives the reader to look beyond every Solomon, however wise, to the true Son of David whose heart never turns and whose kingdom will never be torn away.

sermon.title: Heart Failure
sermon.why: This sermon expounds 1 Kings 11:1-13, the heart of this movement, tracing exactly how Solomon's wisdom did not prevent his heart from being turned away by disordered love, and drawing out the sober warning for believers who assume spiritual maturity guarantees continued faithfulness.

quiz.q: What ultimately turned Solomon's heart away from wholehearted devotion to the LORD?

options.text: His many foreign wives, taken in direct violation of God's command, turned his heart after other gods as he grew old, vv1-4.
options.note: The text states this explicitly and repeatedly across verses 1 through 4.

options.text: His enormous wealth alone, apart from any relational or spiritual cause.
options.note: Wealth surrounds the narrative, but the text specifically names his wives, not wealth itself, as the direct cause of his heart turning.

options.text: Pressure from foreign kings threatening war if he did not worship their gods.
options.note: No such coercion is mentioned; the text describes Solomon clinging to these women in love, not acting under military threat.

options.text: Nothing; the text says Solomon remained wholeheartedly devoted to the LORD his entire life.
options.note: Verse 4 explicitly says he was not wholeheartedly devoted to the LORD, unlike his father David.

quiz.q: How does the LORD temper His judgment on Solomon's kingdom with mercy?

options.text: He delays the tearing of the kingdom until after Solomon's death and preserves one tribe for his son, for David's sake and Jerusalem's, vv12-13.
options.note: This is the explicit mercy the LORD announces alongside His judgment.

options.text: He cancels the judgment entirely once Solomon shows remorse.
options.note: No remorse or cancellation is recorded; the judgment stands, only delayed and partially limited.

options.text: He punishes Solomon's whole family line immediately and completely.
options.note: The opposite is true: judgment is delayed a generation and one tribe is spared, not an immediate, total punishment.

options.text: He shows no mercy at all in this announcement.
options.note: Verses 12-13 explicitly build in mercy for David's sake and for the sake of Jerusalem.

quiz.q: What is the significance of Ahijah tearing his new cloak into twelve pieces before Jeroboam?

options.text: It is a prophetic sign-act announcing that ten tribes will be torn from Solomon's son and given to Jeroboam, while one remains for David's sake, vv30-32.
options.note: Ahijah explains the sign himself in verses 31-32, tying the torn pieces directly to the coming division of the kingdom.

options.text: It is simply a personal gift Ahijah gives Jeroboam with no symbolic meaning.
options.note: The cloak is real, but its tearing is explicitly explained as a prophetic sign of the coming division, not a mere gift.

options.text: It shows Ahijah's anger at Jeroboam for rebelling against Solomon.
options.note: The sign-act communicates God's coming judgment and promise, not anger directed at Jeroboam personally.

options.text: It predicts that Jeroboam will fail to become king at all.
options.note: The prophecy explicitly promises Jeroboam will reign over ten tribes, v31 and v37.

quiz.q: Why does the LORD raise up adversaries like Hadad and Rezon against Solomon in this chapter?

options.text: As part of God's response to Solomon's covenant unfaithfulness, showing His sovereignty over history even while these men act from their own motives.
options.note: The text places these adversaries directly after God's announcement of judgment in verses 9-13, connecting them to Solomon's unfaithfulness.

options.text: Because Solomon had personally wronged Hadad and Rezon and they sought revenge unrelated to any divine purpose.
options.note: Their grievances trace back to David's earlier actions, not Solomon directly, and the text frames their rise as the LORD's doing.

options.text: Because Israel's neighboring nations had always been at war with Solomon from the start of his reign.
options.note: Chapters 4 through 10 describe a peaceful, prosperous reign; these adversaries arise later, tied to the events of chapter 11.

options.text: The text gives no reason at all for these adversaries appearing.
options.note: The narrative explicitly says the LORD raised up each adversary, situating their rise within the chapter's larger theme of judgment.

quiz.q: How long did Solomon reign, and what happened after his death?

options.text: He reigned forty years in Jerusalem, then died and was buried in the city of David, and his son Rehoboam reigned in his place, vv42-43.
options.note: This is the chapter's closing summary, matching the standard regnal formula used throughout Kings.

options.text: He reigned twenty years before being deposed by Jeroboam during his lifetime.
options.note: The LORD explicitly says the kingdom will not be torn away during Solomon's lifetime, v12; he reigned forty years and died peacefully.

options.text: He reigned until his death but left no successor, ending his dynasty completely.
options.note: Verse 43 names Rehoboam as his successor; the Davidic line continues, though soon over a divided kingdom.

options.text: The text says nothing about how his reign ended.
options.note: Verses 41-43 give an explicit closing account of the length of his reign, his death, and his successor.

questions: Solomon's heart was turned not in a single moment but gradually, as he grew old. Where in your own life might small compromises be slowly turning your heart away from wholehearted devotion to God?
The LORD warned Solomon explicitly, twice, before judgment fell. How do you respond when God's word warns you plainly about a pattern in your life?
God's mercy preserved one tribe for David's sake, even amid real judgment. Where have you experienced God's mercy tempering consequences you otherwise deserved?
Solomon possessed unmatched wisdom yet still let disordered love reshape his worship. What safeguards help keep your affections rightly ordered toward God rather than toward what competes with Him?
Unlike Solomon, Christ's heart never turned. How does resting in Christ's perfect faithfulness, rather than your own, change the way you face your own failures and seasons of decline?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes Milcom is a variant of Molech, the same deity named again in verse 7 and verse 33; see Leviticus 18:21 for the law's condemnation of this Ammonite worship.
'Supporting terraces' renders the Hebrew word for the Millo, a structural feature of Jerusalem's fortifications whose precise nature is debated but which Solomon evidently expanded.

The BSB footnote observes the Hebrew literally reads 'he has' forsaken Me, referring to Solomon individually, though some ancient versions read the plural 'they have,' extending the charge to the nation; either reading keeps Solomon's own unfaithfulness central to the judgment.

Shishak king of Egypt, who shelters Jeroboam, is a historically attested pharaoh who later invades Judah in the reign of Rehoboam, 1 Kings 14:25-26, tying this chapter forward into the next generation's troubles.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 11:1-43. Em dashes in the source were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in this file.
